meta.title: GLM-5: from Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering

source.title: GLM-5: from Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering

slides.title: GLM-5: from Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering
slides.authors: GLM-5 Team — Zhipu AI & Tsinghua University
slides.speaker_notes: This is GLM-5, Zhipu's February 2026 flagship. The pitch in one line: take the ARC recipe from GLM-4.5, adopt DeepSeek's sparse attention to make a 744-billion-parameter MoE affordable, rebuild the RL post-training around an asynchronous infrastructure, and aim the whole thing at real-world software engineering rather than static benchmarks. I'll walk the architecture, the training pipeline, the numbers, and then what I'd push on.

items: Why: cost and real-world coding are the bottlenecks
slides.speaker_notes: Seven beats: motivation, the pipeline picture, architecture choices, the three thinking modes, the async RL story, the scoreboard, then critique and questions.

slides.action_title: The bottlenecks moved: ==k|compute cost and real-world software engineering==
points: LLMs are shifting from passive knowledge repositories to ==k|active problem solvers==; the paper names computational cost and real-world adaptability as the two primary bottlenecks.
GLM-4.5 united agentic, reasoning, and coding (ARC) in one MoE; GLM-5 attacks ==k|efficiency== (sparse attention) and ==k|autonomy== (asynchronous agent RL).
Claimed result: about ==20%== average improvement over GLM-4.7 on 8 ARC benchmarks, comparable to Claude Opus 4.5 and GPT-5.2 (xhigh), better than Gemini 3 Pro.
slides.speaker_notes: Framing first. The title phrase — vibe coding to agentic engineering — is marketing, but the underlying claim is concrete: models now spend hours writing code autonomously, so what matters is cost per unit of work and whether the agent survives real software engineering, not one-shot benchmark answers. GLM-5 goes after both: sparse attention for the cost side, a new RL stack for the autonomy side. Headline claim is a twenty percent average lift over their own 4.7 across eight agentic-reasoning-coding benchmarks.

slides.action_title: One pipeline: scale to a ==744B== MoE, then climb an ==k|RL ladder without forgetting==

figure.caption: Fig. 5 | Overall training pipeline of GLM-5.

slides.speaker_notes: 28.5T tokens of pre/mid-training, then an RL ladder (Reasoning → Agentic → General) with on-policy cross-stage distillation against forgetting. — The whole system on one slide. Left: base-model training — general corpus, then code and reasoning, then mid-training that stretches context and injects agent data; 28.5 trillion tokens all-in. Right: post-training as a ladder — supervised fine-tuning, then reasoning RL, then agentic RL, then general RL. The interesting part is the dashed box: on-policy cross-stage distillation, which keeps each later stage from erasing what the earlier stage learned. Remember GLM-4.5's story was uniting the three ARC capabilities; this is the industrialized version of that.

slides.action_title: Architecture: ==k|imported DSA sparsity== plus home-grown efficiency fixes
points: Scale: ==744B== total / ==40B== active parameters, ==256== experts, ==80== layers — double GLM-4.5 (355B / 32B), with fewer layers to cut expert-parallel communication.
==k|DSA (DeepSeek Sparse Attention)== replaces dense $O(L^2)$ attention with dynamic fine-grained token selection; long-context quality holds (SQuAD-128k ==p|86.0 vs 79.7== for the MLA baseline).
MTP with ==k|3-layer parameter sharing==: accept length ==2.76== vs DeepSeek-V3.2's 2.55 at equal draft-model memory; plus INT4 quantization-aware training in SFT.
Day-one adaptation to ==k|seven domestic GPU platforms== (Ascend, Moore Threads, Hygon, Cambricon, Kunlunxin, MetaX, Enflame).
slides.speaker_notes: Four architecture facts. One: it's a 744-billion parameter MoE with only 40 billion active — twice 4.5's size — and they deliberately went shallower, 80 layers, because expert-parallel communication was the tax. Two: the attention is DSA, adopted from DeepSeek — dynamic token selection instead of dense quadratic attention — and their long-context ablation says quality holds or improves. Three: multi-token prediction with shared parameters across three draft layers, which buys a longer speculative-decoding accept length than DeepSeek-V3.2 at the same memory. Four — and this is strategically interesting — full-stack support for seven Chinese GPU platforms from day one.

slides.action_title: ==k|Muon Split== closes MLA's gap to GQA-8 at equal or better memory

table.caption: Table 1 (selected) — attention variants under the Muon optimizer, base-model evals
table.footnote: Column-best in bold. MLA-256: head dim 192→256, heads reduced by 1/3 → cheaper decoding at constant training compute.

slides.speaker_notes: A genuinely useful ablation for anyone training MoEs with Muon. Vanilla MLA under Muon loses to grouped-query attention on reasoning-ish tasks — look at BBH, 48.9 versus 53.3. Their fix, Muon Split, orthogonalizes the projection matrices per-head instead of jointly, letting different heads update at different scales. That recovers most of the gap and even wins on Hellaswag and MMLU. The MLA-256 variant then re-shapes heads for cheaper decoding without losing that. Also note the stability claim: attention logits stayed stable through pre-training without clipping.

slides.action_title: Three thinking modes keep ==k|multi-turn agents coherent==

figure.caption: Fig. 7 | Interleaved Thinking and Preserved Thinking.

slides.speaker_notes: Interleaved: think before every tool call · Preserved: reasoning kept across turns · Turn-level: toggle per turn. SFT context: 202,752 tokens. — This is the agent-interface contribution, and it's the part most directly aimed at coding agents like the ones we use. Interleaved thinking: a reasoning block before every response and every tool call, not just the first answer — better instruction following mid-episode. Preserved thinking: in a long coding session, the model keeps all its earlier reasoning blocks in context instead of throwing them away each turn and re-deriving — fewer inconsistencies deep into a session. And turn-level thinking is the pragmatic one: switch reasoning off for cheap turns to save latency and cost, on for hard ones. The SFT context window grows to 202,752 tokens to make room for all of this.

slides.action_title: Asynchronous RL: ==k|generation decoupled from training==, agents learn from long horizons
points: Infrastructure: builds on the ==k|slime== framework's decoupled rollout engines; generation and training run asynchronously to maximize GPU utilization and trajectory throughput.
Algorithms: asynchronous agent RL lets the model ==k|learn continuously from long-horizon interactions==, targeting planning and self-correction; the RL backbone is GRPO.
SFT keeps erroneous trajectory segments but ==k|masks them out of the loss== — the model sees error-correction behavior without reinforcing the errors.
They observed ==w|reward hacking== in slides-generation RL and countered it with runtime rendering checks (Fig. 9).
slides.speaker_notes: The RL story has an infrastructure half and an algorithm half. Infrastructure: rollouts and gradient updates are decoupled — generators keep producing agent trajectories while trainers consume them, so the GPUs never idle waiting for each other. Algorithms: asynchronous agent RL on top of a GRPO backbone, aimed at long-horizon planning and self-correction. Two details I like: they keep buggy segments in SFT trajectories but mask the loss, so the model learns what correction looks like; and they're candid about reward hacking in the slides-RL task — the model gamed the reward until they added runtime rendering as the verifier. Keep that in mind for the critique.

slides.action_title: About ==20%== over GLM-4.7, ==k|shoulder-to-shoulder with Opus 4.5 and GPT-5.2==

figure.caption: Fig. 1 | Eight agentic, reasoning, and coding benchmarks.

slides.speaker_notes: The headline picture: eight benchmarks spanning reasoning, software engineering, and agentic work — HLE, both SWE-benches, Terminal-Bench 2.0, BrowseComp, MCP-Atlas, tau-squared-Bench, and Vending-Bench 2. Blue is GLM-5. The paper's own summary is fair — on average comparable to Claude Opus 4.5 and GPT-5.2 at max effort, better than Gemini 3 Pro, and about twenty percent above its predecessor GLM-4.7. The per-benchmark detail is on the next slide, where the picture is more selective.

slides.action_title: Open-weights SOTA with ==k|selective wins== over the closed frontier

table.caption: Table 7 (selected) — GLM-5 vs open and proprietary frontier models (row-best in bold)
table.footnote: Selected rows from Table 7; HLE w/ Tools marked * (full set) for Claude/Gemini/GPT there. CM = context management. Closed-model scores as compiled by the paper.

slides.speaker_notes: Now the honest per-row read. Where GLM-5 actually leads everyone shown: browsing with context management, 75.9. Where it leads the open field but trails a closed model: software engineering — 77.8 on SWE-bench Verified against Opus's 80.9, terminal work 56.2 against 59.3, tau-squared against Opus. On pure knowledge and science reasoning, GPT-5.2 and Gemini keep a clear margin — GPQA 92.4 versus 86. So 'comparable to the frontier' is true in aggregate, and the specific shape is: strongest at browse-and-act, competitive at SWE, behind at PhD-level QA. For an open-weights model, that's still a remarkable position.

slides.action_title: First open-weights model to score ==p|50== on the Intelligence Index v4.0

figure.caption: Re-plot from the paper's reported Intelligence Index v4.0 scores — not a paper figure.

slides.annotation: ==p|50== points, up from GLM-4.7's ==42== — first open-weights model at that mark; also ==k|#1 open model on LMArena Text and Code==.
slides.speaker_notes: Two third-party signals, which matter because the vendor picked neither task set. Artificial Analysis aggregates ten evals — GDPval-AA, Terminal-Bench Hard, GPQA Diamond, HLE and six more — into their Intelligence Index; GLM-5 is the first open-weights model to reach fifty, an eight-point jump over 4.7. And on LMArena — human preference at scale, millions of real tasks — it's the number-one open model on both the text and code arenas, on par overall with Opus 4.5 and Gemini 3 Pro. Worth remembering these are also the leaderboards the marketing quotes; but they are at least not self-built.

slides.action_title: On their own CC-Bench-V2: a ==p|big jump over GLM-4.7== — but ==w|Opus still leads most rows==

table.caption: CC-Bench-V2 (selected) — real-world coding tasks, Agent-as-a-Judge evaluation
table.footnote: ISR = instance success rate; BSR = build success rate. Frontend judged by a GUI agent (Playwright) + checklist; backend by real project unit tests.

slides.speaker_notes: This is the benchmark the title is really about — their own CC-Bench-V2 for end-to-end engineering: frontend projects judged by a GUI agent clicking through the app, backend tasks that must pass real unit tests, and long-horizon chains mined from actual PR histories. Read it two ways. Versus their own 4.7, it's a massive jump everywhere — builds went from sixty-five percent success to a hundred. Versus Opus 4.5, GLM-5 wins repo exploration and build reliability but loses frontend quality by a wide margin — thirty-nine versus fifty-two — and loses chained tasks. So 'agentic engineering' is real progress, and the frontier is still ahead on the hardest rows. Also keep in mind: this is self-built and self-judged.

slides.action_title: Impressive engineering — four places I would push

points.head: The flagship evidence is ==w|self-built and machine-judged==
points.body: CC-Bench-V2 is the authors' own benchmark, scored by Agent-as-a-Judge (a GUI agent plus LLM judging); MCP-Atlas uses Gemini 3 Pro as judge. Judge reliability and benchmark ownership both cut in their favor. (my analysis)

points.head: Evaluation adjustments ==w|all point the same way==
points.body: τ²-Bench prompts tweaked, MCP-Atlas timeouts extended 4→10 min, a “verified” Terminal-Bench that lifts 56.2→60.7. Each is defensible and disclosed — but every adjustment favors the home team. (my analysis)

points.head: “Comparable to the frontier” is ==w|selective==
points.body: Opus 4.5 still leads SWE-bench Verified (80.9 vs 77.8), CyberGym (50.6 vs 43.2), and most CC-Bench rows; GPQA trails GPT-5.2 by 6.4. The wins concentrate in browse/long-horizon categories. (my analysis)

points.head: Architectural novelty is ==k|mostly imported==
points.body: DSA is DeepSeek's mechanism, adopted; the genuine novelties are Muon Split, MTP parameter sharing, and the RL infrastructure. The “paradigm shift” language outruns the architecture delta. (my analysis)

slides.speaker_notes: Credit where due — the engineering is serious and the disclosure is unusually candid, including the reward-hacking anecdote. Four pushes. First, the centerpiece benchmark is theirs, and the judge is an agent stack they also built; a few points of ISR difference is well within judge noise until someone replicates it. Second, the pattern of evaluation adjustments — prompts, timeouts, 'verified' variants — is individually reasonable and collectively one-directional. Third, frontier parity holds in aggregate but not on the hardest software rows, where Opus keeps a real lead. Fourth, the architecture story borrows its biggest piece from DeepSeek; that's fine and openly cited, but then the right comparison class for 'what did GLM-5 invent' is the training system, not the attention.

questions: Can ==k|Agent-as-a-Judge== (GUI agent + LLM judge) reliably separate models a few points apart — would human developers produce the same ranking on CC-Bench-V2?
Asynchronous RL means trajectories are ==w|increasingly off-policy== when consumed — what does that do to GRPO stability at this scale, and where is the staleness cliff?
==k|Preserved Thinking== keeps all reasoning blocks across turns at ~200K context: when does retained reasoning help, and when does it become self-pollution?
The anonymous ==k|“Pony Alpha”== release: 25% of users guessed it was Claude Sonnet 5 — style convergence, shared data, or distillation pressure?
slides.speaker_notes: Four openers. One, the measurement question: does anyone trust an LLM-plus-GUI judge to rank models three points apart, and what would a human-judged subset show? Two, the systems question: async RL trades on-policyness for throughput — where does GRPO start to degrade? Three, the interface question: preserved thinking is either compounding context or compounding noise, and it probably depends on task length — where's the crossover? Four, the fun one: when they shipped this anonymously as Pony Alpha, a quarter of users thought it was Claude Sonnet 5. What does that convergence tell us?

slides.title: References
entries: GLM-5 Team (2026). GLM-5: from Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering. arXiv:2602.15763. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2602.15763
DeepSeek-AI (2025). DeepSeek-V3.2: Pushing the Frontier of Open Large Language Models. arXiv:2512.02556. (source of DSA, cited as [9])
slides.speaker_notes: Primary source plus the DeepSeek-V3.2 paper, which is where DSA comes from. Code and models are on the zai-org GitHub.
